summary: Khatam al-nabiyyin ("the Seal of the Prophets") is the Islamic doctrine of the finality of Muhammad's prophethood: the belief that he is the last of the prophets and that no prophet comes after him. The title derives from a Qur'anic description of Muhammad (Q 33:40).
description: Khatam al-nabiyyin, the "Seal of the Prophets," is a Qur'anic epithet of Muhammad (Q 33:40) that came to anchor the doctrine of the finality of prophethood: the affirmation that Muhammad is the last prophet God sends and that prophethood is closed after him. In its developed form the doctrine holds a central and undisputed place in Islamic belief, bound up with the completeness of the revelation Muhammad brought and the sufficiency of his community's guidance until the end of time. Modern academic study has argued that this settled consensus emerged over time: the sense of the Qur'anic phrase khatam al-nabiyyin was, on this reading, debated in the first Islamic century, with some early Muslims understanding "seal" to mean that Muhammad confirmed or authenticated the prophets before him and others taking it to mean that he brought the office of prophethood to a close, before the finality reading became authoritative and was reinforced by hadith. The doctrine bears on the boundary of legitimate prophetic claims and has been the reference point in later disputes over claimants to prophethood; those disputes are contemporary and sectarian and lie outside the scope of this entry. Khatam al-nabiyyin is an aspect of the wider doctrine of nubuwwa, prophethood, of which it marks the terminus.
editorial_notes: This entry is scoped to the doctrine of the finality of prophethood as an aspect of nubuwwa (to which it is linked as related_to), not to the contemporary sectarian disputes over particular claimants, which the neutrality policy places out of scope (Out of scope: contemporary political or sectarian disputes framed as theology). The developed doctrine (finality as a central, settled belief) and the modern scholarly account of its historical emergence (an early first-century debate over the meaning of the Qur'anic phrase) are both reported as attributed views, per neutrality principles #1 and #2, without the editor endorsing either the doctrine or the historical thesis. Sourcing (principle #8): the Western academic lens is Yohanan Friedmann's peer-reviewed article, the foundational modern study of the doctrine's history, carried at high confidence for the account of its emergence; a modern Muslim scholarly lens is Fazlur Rahman's Prophecy in Islam. A classical primary text or heresiographical treatment carrying the developed doctrine on the tradition's own terms is flagged as not yet secured; it should be added (e.g. a kalam creed or al-Shahrastani's al-Milal wa-l-Nihal on the boundary of prophethood) before this entry is promoted beyond draft, so the third lens is currently a gap.

relations.note: The finality of prophethood marks the terminus of nubuwwa, the doctrine of prophethood.
The evidentiary miracle authenticates a prophet's claim, the question the finality doctrine closes for Muhammad's successors.
The completed prophetic message safeguards the doctrine of God's oneness.

historical_period.note: Rooted in the Qur'anic epithet (Q 33:40); the finality reading is argued by modern scholarship to have become authoritative over the first Islamic centuries.

sources.citation: Friedmann, Yohanan. "Finality of Prophethood in Sunni Islam." Jerusalem Studies in Arabic and Islam 7 (1986): 177-215. The foundational modern academic study of the doctrine, arguing that the meaning of khatam al-nabiyyin was contested in the first Islamic century (between a 'confirmation/authentication' reading and a 'closure of prophethood' reading) before the finality reading acquired an undisputed and central place in Islamic religious thought.

Rahman, Fazlur. Prophecy in Islam: Philosophy and Orthodoxy. London: George Allen and Unwin, 1958; repr. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1979. A monograph on the doctrine of prophethood (nubuwwa) by a leading modern Muslim scholar, treating the nature and scope of prophethood and its orthodox reception. Added as the modern Muslim scholarly lens; cited at medium confidence as its relevant passages on the finality of prophethood were not read line-by-line here.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.
